# Integration Error Patterns
# Fetched by the standing instructions when an integration error occurs.
# Do not edit this file directly.

INTEGRATION ERROR PATTERNS

Reference this section when an integration setup step fails 
or produces an unexpected error. Match the symptom to a 
pattern below and follow the corresponding fix.

CRITICAL RULE — UNCERTAINTY:
If the error does NOT match any pattern below, do NOT 
fabricate a technical explanation. Specifically, never:
- Invent propagation delays, sync windows, or wait times 
  not documented by the third-party product
- Claim two similar UI elements use different validation 
  rules unless that is explicitly documented
- Offer a confident "the fix is X" when the root cause is 
  unknown to you

When the pattern is unknown, tell the user plainly:
"I don't know why this is failing. The most likely thing 
to try is [single defensible guess], framed as a guess. 
If that doesn't work, I'll save this integration as 
pending so we can come back to it."

Then save as pending and move on. Do not attempt multiple 
sequential "fixes" without acknowledging each is speculative.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PATTERN 1 — GA4 service account email rejected
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Symptom: User tries to add a service account email 
(format: name@project-id.iam.gserviceaccount.com) to GA4 
Property Access Management or Account Access Management. 
Gets the error: "This email doesn't match a Google Account."

Likely root cause: The service account was created in a 
Google Cloud project owned by a personal Gmail account. 
GA4's access management UI often rejects these. Service 
accounts from Workspace-backed GCP projects are accepted 
more reliably.

Note: This is observed behaviour, not formally documented 
by Google. Treat as "the most common cause we've seen" 
rather than absolute fact.

Fix sequence:
1. Check whether the GA4 property already has a service 
   account listed in Account Access Management. If yes, 
   use that one — don't create a new one. Ask the user 
   if they have the JSON key or can get it from whoever 
   set it up.
2. If no existing service account, the new one needs to 
   come from a Workspace-backed GCP project. This often 
   means a shared admin account (commonly tools@ or 
   admin@[company-domain]).
3. If neither option is available right now, save GA4 as 
   PENDING with a clear handoff note: "Service account 
   needed from Workspace-backed GCP project. Currently 
   blocked — [who] needs to provide credentials."

What NOT to say to the user:
- "Wait 2-5 minutes for the service account to propagate" 
  — this is not a real Google behaviour
- "Property Access Management uses different validation 
  than Account Access Management" — they use the same 
  validation; both will reject the same email

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PATTERN 2 — LinkedIn Community Management API approval pending
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Symptom: User has created a LinkedIn developer app and 
requested Community Management API access, but the app 
shows the API as "under review" or "pending approval." 
Cannot generate a valid access token until approved.

Root cause: LinkedIn manually reviews Community Management 
API requests. Typical wait: 3-7 business days. Sometimes 
longer.

Fix sequence:
1. Save LinkedIn as PENDING with the access token expiry 
   date noted as "awaiting API approval."
2. Tell the user: "Nothing to do but wait. LinkedIn 
   reviews these in 3-7 business days. When the approval 
   email arrives, type 'Set up LinkedIn integration' and 
   we'll finish in 2 minutes."
3. Continue setup of other integrations.
4. Do not block the rest of Stage 0D on this.

Prevention: When the asset request is sent at the start 
of the engagement, ask the client to apply for the 
Community Management API immediately so approval lands 
before the build session.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PATTERN 3 — Shared admin account with 2FA blocking access
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Symptom: The credentials needed for an integration (GCP, 
LinkedIn admin, Google Workspace) sit in a shared admin 
account like tools@[company] or admin@[company]. User has 
the password but does not have the 2FA device, which 
typically belongs to someone else (often the original 
setup owner, e.g. Franki, IT lead, ops manager).

Fix sequence:
1. Do NOT instruct the user to attempt the login without 
   knowing where the 2FA prompt will go. An unexpected 
   2FA request is a security red flag and creates 
   organisational confusion.
2. Save the integration as PENDING with a clear handoff 
   note: "Credentials in shared admin account [name]. 2FA 
   coordination needed with [owner] before access."
3. Suggest the user message the 2FA owner asking for 
   either (a) the credentials to be retrieved on their 
   behalf, or (b) a coordinated 30-second window to 
   approve the 2FA prompt while the user logs in.
4. Move to the next integration. Do not block.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PATTERN 4 — Existing service account that's been used previously
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Symptom: When checking the GA4 property's Account Access 
Management page, an existing service account is visible 
that someone at the client set up previously. User is 
unsure whether to use it or create a new one.

Fix: Always use the existing one. Reasons:
- It's already been approved and added to GA4
- Avoids cluttering the account with redundant service 
  accounts
- Avoids the email rejection pattern from PATTERN 1

To get the JSON key for an existing service account:
1. User needs access to the GCP project that owns the 
   service account. If they don't have access, see 
   PATTERN 3 (shared admin account).
2. In the GCP project: IAM & Admin → Service Accounts → 
   click the existing account → Keys tab → Add Key → 
   Create new key → JSON → Create.
3. Note: existing keys cannot be re-downloaded. A new 
   key must be created. Creating a new key does not 
   invalidate the existing key — both will work.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PATTERN 5 — LinkedIn requires two separate apps for Community + Ads
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Symptom: User has set up a LinkedIn developer app with 
Community Management API and is trying to add Ads API 
scopes to the same app. LinkedIn does not allow both 
product groups on the same app.

Fix: Confirm to the user this is correct LinkedIn 
behaviour, not a setup error. They need two separate 
apps:
- App 1: Community Management API (for analytics and 
  organic posting)
- App 2: Ads API (for paid campaigns and reporting)

Each app generates its own access token. Store both in 
integrations.md under separate sections:
- ## LinkedIn Analytics (Community app credentials)
- ## LinkedIn Ads (Ads app credentials)

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PATTERN 6 — User cannot access the GCP project that owns the existing service account
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Symptom: An existing GA4 service account is visible in 
Account Access Management but the user's GCP project list 
does not include the project that owns it (e.g. they can 
see "ga4-mcp-service@tg-ga4-mcp.iam.gserviceaccount.com" 
in GA4 but "tg-ga4-mcp" is not in their GCP project list).

Root cause: The GCP project was created under a different 
Google account — typically a shared admin account or a 
former employee's account.

Fix sequence:
1. Identify the likely owner. The project name often 
   contains a clue (tg-ga4-mcp suggests Think & Grow 
   shared admin setup).
2. Determine whether the user has access to the shared 
   admin account that owns it (see PATTERN 3 for 2FA 
   considerations).
3. If yes, log in to the shared admin account, switch 
   to the relevant GCP project, generate a new JSON key 
   from the existing service account, save securely.
4. If no, save GA4 as PENDING with a handoff note 
   identifying the likely owner.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────


─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PATTERN 7 — HubSpot Private App token rejected (401)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Symptom: HubSpot API call returns 401 Unauthorized 
immediately after the token is pasted and saved.

Likely causes in order of probability:
1. Token was truncated on paste — HubSpot Private App 
   tokens are long (starts with pat-na1- or pat-eu1- 
   followed by ~50 characters). Partial pastes fail silently.
2. Token was rotated — if someone regenerated the Private 
   App token after it was created, the old token is 
   immediately invalid.
3. Private App was deleted — the app was removed in 
   HubSpot settings, invalidating all its tokens.

Fix sequence:
1. Ask the human to confirm the token they pasted starts 
   with pat-na1- or pat-eu1-. If not, it's the wrong 
   credential type (not a Private App token).
2. Ask them to go back to HubSpot → Settings → 
   Integrations → Private Apps and confirm the app 
   still exists and is active.
3. If the app exists: click "Show token" — copy again 
   carefully and repaste.
4. If the app was deleted or cannot be found: create a 
   new Private App following the setup guide above.
5. If still failing after a fresh token: save as PENDING 
   and flag for HubSpot admin to investigate.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PATTERN 8 — HubSpot 403 — missing scopes
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Symptom: HubSpot API call returns 403 Forbidden. 
Token is valid but the call is rejected.

Root cause: The Private App was created without the 
required scopes. HubSpot scope permissions are set 
at app creation and can be edited afterward.

Required scopes for this system:
  crm.objects.contacts.read — list size and growth
  marketing-email — campaign performance (opens, clicks)
  content — landing pages and blog if hosted in HubSpot

Fix:
1. In HubSpot: Settings → Integrations → Private Apps →
   click the app → Scopes tab.
2. Add the missing scope(s).
3. Save. The token does not change — no need to repaste.
4. Retry the API call.

If you encounter an error pattern not listed above:
1. Acknowledge to the user that this is not a known 
   pattern.
2. Suggest ONE defensible next step (framed as a guess).
3. Offer to save the integration as PENDING.
4. Add the new pattern to a working note so it can be 
   added to this section in a future product update.

---

SKILL FALLBACKS

These are condensed fallback versions of each skill used in
this system. They activate automatically if a live skill
fetch fails. They produce acceptable output — not full-skill
output — and are a safety net, not a replacement for the
live skills. The forked repo should always be the primary source.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FALLBACK: customer-research
Used in: Stage 0A (audience file check)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Review the audience.md file and check for:
- Is the ICP defined by role, industry, company size, 
  and seniority — not just vague demographics?
- Are the buyer's primary pain points stated in their 
  own language, not the company's language?
- Is there a clear trigger event — what causes this 
  person to start looking for a solution?
- Are alternatives and objections named?
- Is there a distinction between the buyer and the user
  if they are different people?

Add anything genuinely missing. If the file is complete,
make no changes.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FALLBACK: seo-audit
Used in: Stage 0B (website audit — SEO section)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
For the client's website, assess and report on:

Title tags and meta descriptions:
- Is the primary keyword in the title tag?
- Is the title under 60 characters?
- Is the meta description under 155 characters with a CTA?

Heading structure:
- Is there exactly one H1 per page?
- Do H2s and H3s reflect the page's keyword themes?

Page speed:
- Fetch via PageSpeed Insights API if available
- If not available, note as requiring manual check

Content quality:
- Do pages have sufficient depth for their target keyword?
- Is there duplicate or thin content?

Internal linking:
- Do service/product pages link to each other logically?
- Is the homepage linking to the most important pages?

Mobile:
- Is the site responsive? Check viewport meta tag.

Indexing:
- Is there a robots.txt? Does it block anything it shouldn't?
- Is there a sitemap.xml?

Score each area: Good / Needs work / Critical issue
Provide one actionable recommendation per area that needs work.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FALLBACK: ai-seo
Used in: Stage 0B (website audit — AI SEO section)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Assess the client's website for AI search visibility:

Content structure:
- Are key pages written in direct, answer-style prose
  that an AI could extract and cite?
- Are there clear definitions of what the company does,
  who it serves, and what problems it solves?
- Does the site use question-and-answer formatting
  anywhere? (FAQ sections, headers phrased as questions)

Authority signals:
- Are there statistics, original data, or research the
  site owns that an AI might cite?
- Are there named experts with credentials?
- Are there clear About and contact pages?

Schema markup:
- Note whether structured data is likely present
  (cannot be confirmed via fetch — flag for manual check)

Recommendations:
- Identify the top 2-3 pages most likely to be cited
  in AI search results for this company's key queries
- Note what would make each one more citable

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FALLBACK: site-architecture
Used in: Stage 0B (website audit — site structure section)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Assess the client's site structure:

Navigation:
- Is the primary nav clear and limited to 5-7 items?
- Can a visitor find Services/Products, About, and
  Contact within one click from the homepage?
- Is there a clear primary CTA visible on every page?

Page hierarchy:
- Does the site have a logical top/mid/bottom tier?
  (Homepage → Category pages → Specific pages)
- Are there orphan pages with no internal links?

URL structure:
- Are URLs descriptive and human-readable?
- Is the structure consistent across similar page types?

Key missing pages:
- Is there a dedicated page for each core service?
- Is there a case study or social proof section?
- Is there a resources or blog section?

Provide a brief recommended structure if significant
issues are found.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FALLBACK: page-cro
Used in: Stage 0B (website audit — CRO section)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
For the homepage and up to two key service pages:

Value proposition (5-second test):
- Does the above-the-fold content answer: what is this,
  who is it for, and why should I care?
- Is the primary benefit specific, or is it vague?

CTAs:
- Is there a single clear primary CTA per page?
- Is the CTA specific ("Book a free call") or generic
  ("Learn more", "Click here")?
- Does the CTA appear above the fold?

Trust signals:
- Are there client logos, testimonials, or case studies?
- Are they specific (named clients, real numbers)?

Friction:
- Is the contact/conversion process clear?
- How many clicks to reach the primary CTA's destination?

Score each page: Strong / Average / Needs work
One recommendation per page that needs work.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FALLBACK: form-cro
Used in: Stage 0B (website audit — form section)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
For any lead capture or contact forms found on the site:

Field count:
- How many fields are visible?
- Are there fields that are unlikely to affect follow-up
  quality? (e.g. phone number on a low-intent form)
- Ideal for a lead form: name, email, one context field

Labels and microcopy:
- Are field labels above the field, not inside it?
- Is there any copy explaining what happens after submit?

CTA button:
- Is the submit button specific ("Get my free audit")
  or generic ("Submit", "Send")?

Trust:
- Is there a privacy note near the email field?

Flag forms that have more than 4 fields, generic submit
buttons, or no post-submit explanation.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FALLBACK: analytics-tracking
Used in: Stage 0B (website audit — analytics section)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Assess analytics setup based on what is visible or inferable:

What to check:
- Is GA4 or another analytics tool likely installed?
  (Check for gtag, analytics scripts in page source if
  accessible — flag as requiring manual confirmation if not)
- Is there a cookie consent banner? (Suggests analytics
  present and some compliance awareness)
- Is conversion tracking likely set up?
  (Cannot confirm without account access — note as TBC)

Minimum viable tracking checklist:
- GA4 installed and collecting sessions
- Contact form submission tracked as a conversion event
- LinkedIn Insight Tag installed if running LinkedIn Ads
- UTM parameters used consistently in any outbound links

If integrations.md shows GA4 as connected, note the
above as likely in place. If not connected, include
"Set up GA4 tracking" as a recommended action.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FALLBACK: competitor-profiling
Used in: Stage 0B (competitor analysis section)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
For each competitor in competitors.md, fetch their
homepage via r.jina.ai/[competitor-url] and produce:

Company summary:
- One paragraph: what they do, who they serve, how they
  position themselves

Messaging:
- Their headline value proposition (direct quote if visible)
- Primary differentiator claim
- Tone: formal / conversational / technical / aspirational

Content and channels:
- Primary content channel (LinkedIn, blog, YouTube, etc.)
- Posting frequency if visible
- Content themes or formats they favour

Strengths vs the client:
- 2-3 areas where they appear stronger than the client

Weaknesses / gaps:
- 2-3 areas the client could credibly outperform them

Format as a scannable reference. Keep each profile to
under 300 words. Save combined output to
/02-audit/competitive-brief.md.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FALLBACK: lead-magnets
Used in: Stage 0B (lead generation audit section)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Assess lead magnet and email capture for the client:

Current state:
- Is there a visible lead magnet on the site?
  (checklist, guide, template, free tool, newsletter)
- Is there an email capture form that isn't just contact?
- What is the implied value exchange?

Quality check:
- Is the lead magnet specific to a pain point the
  audience actually has?
- Does it deliver immediate value without requiring
  a sales conversation?
- Is the format appropriate for the audience?
  (Executives: short frameworks. Practitioners: templates)

Gap analysis:
- If no lead magnet exists: recommend one format and
  topic based on the audience.md and content-strategy.md
- If a lead magnet exists but is weak: note why and
  suggest one specific improvement

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FALLBACK: copy-editing
Used in: Stage 2 (draft quality check)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Review the draft post copy through five passes:

Pass 1 — Clarity:
- Is every sentence immediately understandable?
- Remove or rewrite anything that needs to be re-read

Pass 2 — Concision:
- Remove filler words: very, really, just, quite, 
  that, in order to, the fact that
- Break sentences over 25 words into two

Pass 3 — Voice:
- Does this sound like the brand-voice.md profile?
- Is the tone consistent throughout — no formal opening
  followed by a casual sign-off?

Pass 4 — Hook:
- Does the first line earn the second line?
- Would this stop a scroll? If not, rewrite the
  opening line only

Pass 5 — CTA:
- Is there one clear action for the reader to take?
- Is it specific? ("Comment your answer below" not
  "Let me know your thoughts")

Return the edited copy with a one-line note on what
changed in each pass. If no changes needed in a pass,
note "Pass [#]: clean."

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FALLBACK: marketing-psychology
Used in: Stage 2 (draft quality check)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Review the draft post for psychological effectiveness:

Social proof: Does the post reference results, clients,
or outcomes that make the claim credible? If not, flag.

Specificity: Are claims specific (numbers, names, 
timeframes) or generic? Specific claims are more
persuasive. Flag any generic claims.

Loss aversion: Does the post frame around what the
reader risks missing or losing, not just what they gain?
If appropriate for the content, suggest a reframe.

Relevance: Is the opening line directly relevant to
something the target audience experiences or worries
about? If it starts with the company rather than the
audience, flag.

One improvement: Based on the above, suggest one
specific edit that would increase the persuasive
impact of the post. One only — not a list.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FALLBACK: paid-ads
Used in: Stage 4 (paid ads analysis)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Analyse the LinkedIn Ads data from the API response:

Performance benchmarks for LinkedIn Ads (B2B):
- CTR: good > 0.5%, average 0.3-0.5%, poor < 0.3%
- CPM: varies by audience — flag if > $50
- Conversion rate: good > 3%, average 1-3%, poor < 1%
- CPC: good < $8, average $8-15, poor > $15 for B2B

For each active campaign, assess:
- CTR vs benchmark — improving, declining, or flat?
- CPC trend — is cost per click rising?
- Top performing ad — what format and message?
- Underperforming ads — what do they have in common?

Budget allocation:
- Is spend concentrated on the best-performing campaign?
- Is any budget going to campaigns below benchmark
  on all metrics?

Recommendations:
- One action per underperforming campaign
- One action to scale what is working
- Flag any campaign that should be paused

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FALLBACK: ad-creative
Used in: Stage 4 (paid ads analysis — creative review)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Review the ad creative in the LinkedIn Ads data:

For each ad variant, note:
- Format: single image / carousel / video / text
- Headline: is it benefit-focused or feature-focused?
- Copy length: is the intro text under 150 characters?
  (LinkedIn truncates longer text)
- CTA button: is it appropriate for the offer?

Creative fatigue check:
- How many days has each ad been running?
- Is CTR declining week-over-week? (Sign of fatigue)
- Flag any ad running more than 60 days with declining CTR

Improvement suggestions (top two only):
- For the lowest-performing ad: one specific headline rewrite
- For the highest-performing ad: suggest one variation
  to test against it

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
FALLBACK: ab-test-setup
Used in: Stage 4 (paid ads analysis — test recommendations)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Based on the ads performance data, recommend one A/B test:

Test selection criteria:
- Choose the variable with the most performance variance
  across current ads (headline, image, CTA, audience)
- Do not recommend testing something already being tested

Test design:
- Hypothesis: "We believe that changing [X] to [Y] will
  improve [metric] because [reason based on data]"
- Variable: exactly one change between control and variant
- Success metric: CTR, conversion rate, or CPA — pick one
- Minimum run time: need at least 1,000 impressions per 
  variant before drawing conclusions

Output format:
- Control: [describe existing ad]
- Variant: [describe the single change]
- Hypothesis: [one sentence]
- Success metric: [one metric with a target]
- Minimum run time: [days based on impression volume]
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
